section.title: God Judges the Rulers
section.sectionRationale: A short, sharp Asaphite courtroom scene in which God takes His stand in the divine assembly and indicts human judges, called 'gods' because they exercise delegated authority, for failing to defend the weak. It closes with a plea for God Himself to judge the whole earth.
section.thesis: Every human authority holds delegated, borrowed power under God's own judgment, and true justice means defending the weak, the fatherless, and the oppressed, a standard only God's own righteous reign, previewed in this psalm and claimed by Jesus, can finally and fully satisfy.
section.frameNote: Five scenes: the Judge arrives, 1; the charges of unjust favoritism, 2-4; the diagnosis of moral blindness, 5; the verdict of mortality on unfaithful rulers, 6-7; and the closing appeal for God to judge the whole earth, 8.

units.label: The Judge arrives
units.note: God presides in the divine assembly

verses.text: God presides in the divine assembly; He renders judgment among the {{elohim|gods}}:
"How long will you {{shaphat|judge}} unjustly and show partiality to the wicked? Selah
Defend the cause of the weak and fatherless; uphold the rights of the afflicted and oppressed.
Rescue the weak and needy; save them from the hand of the wicked.
They do not know or understand; they wander in the darkness; all the foundations of the earth are shaken.

units.label: The verdict
units.note: sons of the Most High will die like mortals

verses.text: I have said, 'You are {{elohim|gods}}; you are all sons of the Most High.'
But like mortals you will die, and like rulers you will fall."

units.label: The appeal
units.note: arise, O God, judge the earth

verses.text: Arise, O God, judge the earth, for all the nations are Your inheritance.

elohim.gloss: God, or, applied to human judges, 'gods,' meaning those who exercise God-given authority to judge. The same word names both the LORD presiding, verse 1, and the corrupt human rulers He judges, verse 6, a wordplay Jesus cites in John 10:34 to defend His own claim to divine sonship.

shaphat.gloss: To judge, govern, or vindicate. God 'renders judgment' among the gods and commands them to 'judge' justly, verse 2, showing that all human judgment is meant to mirror His own righteous rule.

basic.context: In a vivid courtroom scene, God stands in the divine assembly and puts human judges, called 'gods' because they hold delegated authority, on trial for favoring the wicked and neglecting the weak, fatherless, afflicted, and oppressed. He pronounces that despite their exalted title, they will die like any mortal, and the psalm closes with a plea for God Himself to judge the whole earth.
basic.hermeneutics: The 'gods' here are not other deities but human judges or rulers, echoing Exodus 22:28's use of the same word for those who exercise judicial authority. The psalm indicts the abuse of real, God-given authority, not competing divine beings, and calls for God's own justice to prevail where human justice fails.
basic.originalLanguages: The word elohim names both the LORD who presides in judgment, verse 1, and the human authorities He judges, verse 6, since they exercise a derived, God-given office to shaphat, judge, on His behalf, an office they have corrupted through partiality.
basic.typology: The psalm's picture of God rising to judge the earth with perfect justice anticipates the final, universal judgment and reign of Christ, who alone judges without partiality and vindicates the oppressed completely.
basic.christ: Jesus quotes this psalm directly, John 10:34-36, arguing that if Scripture calls flawed human judges 'gods' because they bear a derived divine commission, it is no blasphemy for Him, sent and set apart by the Father, to call Himself God's Son.
basic.god: God is shown as the supreme Judge who holds every human authority accountable to His own standard of justice for the weak, and who will Himself rise to judge the earth perfectly and finally when human justice fails.
basic.crossReferences: John 10:34-36 shows Jesus using this psalm's language to defend His identity as the Son of God. Isaiah 3:13-15 pictures the LORD prosecuting leaders who crush His people, echoing this psalm's courtroom scene. Revelation 11:15 answers this psalm's closing plea, when the kingdoms of the world become the kingdom of the Lord.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's courtroom scene, exposing corrupt human authority and pleading for God's own righteous judgment over the earth, points forward to Christ, who both claims the divine authority this psalm implies and will one day judge the earth in perfect, final justice.

context.literary: A tightly structured courtroom drama in five brief scenes: the Judge takes His stand, the charges are laid, the diagnosis of blindness is given, the verdict of mortality is pronounced, and the psalm closes with a prayer for universal judgment.
context.historical: Fits the setting of Israel's court system, where elders and judges sat at city gates to settle disputes, Deuteronomy 16:18; the psalm confronts those officeholders, whether local judges or the nation's rulers, for abandoning justice for the vulnerable.
context.canonical: A key text behind the biblical concern for defending the fatherless, widow, and poor, echoed in Isaiah 1:17, Micah 6:8, and James 1:27, and uniquely significant as the passage Jesus cites to explain how Scripture can call others 'gods' without contradicting monotheism.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confront human judges and rulers for abusing their God-given authority through partiality toward the wicked, to remind them that their exalted office does not exempt them from mortality and accountability, and to plead for God's own righteous judgment over all the earth.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific indictment of corrupt judges in Israel's own courts; it prescribes as an abiding standard that every human authority must defend the weak, fatherless, afflicted, and oppressed, and remains accountable to God regardless of title or power.

debates.question: Who are the 'gods' addressed in this psalm?

views.label: Human judges or rulers, exercising a God-given office of judgment
views.case: Exodus 22:28 uses the same word for human judges, and the psalm's charges, favoring the wicked, failing to defend the weak, fit human courts rather than heavenly beings, and Jesus reads it this way in John 10:34.

views.label: Angelic or spiritual powers permitted authority over the nations, now called to account
views.case: The cosmic language, 'foundations of the earth are shaken,' and the address 'sons of the Most High,' a phrase used of angelic beings elsewhere, Job 1:6, 38:7, suggests spiritual powers behind unjust human rule are also in view.

debates.bearing: Either way, the psalm insists that every authority, human or spiritual, holds power only by God's permission and remains fully accountable to His justice.

study.originalLanguages: The single word elohim names both the LORD presiding as Judge, verse 1, and the human authorities on trial, verse 6, because they hold a derived commission to shaphat, judge, on His behalf, a commission they have betrayed through partiality toward the wicked.

typology.type: God rising to judge the earth in perfect justice, verse 8
typology.fulfillment: The final judgment and righteous reign of Christ, who judges without partiality and fully vindicates the oppressed, Revelation 11:15.

study.christ: Jesus cites Psalm 82:6 directly in John 10:34-36, reasoning that if Scripture itself calls flawed human authorities 'gods' because of a derived commission from God's word, His own claim to be the Son of God, sent and consecrated by the Father, cannot be dismissed as blasphemy. The psalm's closing plea for God to judge the earth anticipates Christ's own future, perfect judgment of all nations.
study.god: God is the supreme Judge over every human court and ruler, holding all delegated authority accountable to His standard of defending the weak, fatherless, afflicted, and oppressed, and He will one day rise to judge the whole earth Himself when human justice fails.

crossReferences.note: Jesus uses this psalm's language about 'gods' to defend His identity as the Son of God.
Pictures the LORD prosecuting leaders who crush His people, echoing this psalm's courtroom scene.
Answers this psalm's closing plea, as the kingdoms of the world become the kingdom of the Lord.

study.oneStory: Psalm 82 exposes corrupt human authority in God's own courtroom and pleads for Him to rise and judge the whole earth. That plea is answered in Christ, who both claims the very authority this psalm assumes and will one day judge all nations in perfect, final righteousness.

sermon.title: Putting the Gods in Their Place
sermon.why: Piper works through Psalm 82 verse by verse, explaining the 'gods' as delegated powers standing under God's judgment and showing how the psalm's closing plea, 'arise, O God, judge the earth,' anchors hope in God's ultimate ownership and justice over all nations.

quiz.q: Where does God take His stand in this psalm, verse 1?

options.text: In the divine assembly, rendering judgment among the gods.
options.note: Verse 1 sets this exact courtroom scene.

options.text: In a private meeting with only the king.
options.note: The scene is a public, assembly-wide judgment, not a private royal audience.

options.text: Nowhere; the psalm describes no such scene.
options.note: Verse 1 explicitly describes God presiding in judgment.

options.text: In a foreign nation's temple.
options.note: The setting is the divine assembly over the 'gods,' not a foreign temple.

quiz.q: What charge does God bring against the 'gods,' verses 2-4?

options.text: Judging unjustly and showing partiality to the wicked instead of defending the weak, fatherless, afflicted, and oppressed.
options.note: This is the exact charge laid out in verses 2-4.

options.text: Failing to build enough temples.
options.note: The charge concerns justice for the vulnerable, not temple construction.

options.text: Collecting too many taxes from the wealthy.
options.note: The concern is partiality toward the wicked and neglect of the weak, not taxation policy.

options.text: Refusing to go to war.
options.note: The charge is about unjust judgment, not military decisions.

quiz.q: What is the effect of the 'gods' moral blindness, verse 5?

options.text: All the foundations of the earth are shaken.
options.note: Verse 5 states this consequence directly.

options.text: The nation becomes wealthier and more stable.
options.note: The described effect is instability and shaking, not prosperity.

options.text: Nothing changes at all.
options.note: The text describes a real, destabilizing effect, not the absence of consequence.

options.text: Only the judges themselves are affected.
options.note: The effect described is cosmic and societal, 'foundations of the earth,' not limited to the judges alone.

quiz.q: What verdict does God pronounce on the 'gods' despite their exalted title, verses 6-7?

options.text: Though called 'sons of the Most High,' they will die like mortals and fall like any ruler.
options.note: Verses 6-7 pronounce exactly this verdict of mortality.

options.text: They will be granted eternal life regardless of their conduct.
options.note: The verdict is mortality and fall, the opposite of unconditional eternal reward.

options.text: They will be promoted to even higher office.
options.note: The verdict is a humbling fall, not a promotion.

options.text: They will be forgiven without any consequence.
options.note: The text pronounces a real verdict of mortality, not an absence of consequence.

quiz.q: How does Jesus use this psalm in John 10:34-36?

options.text: He cites verse 6 to argue that if Scripture calls human authorities 'gods,' His own claim to be the Son of God is not blasphemy.
options.note: This is exactly how Jesus reasons from this psalm in John 10.

options.text: He uses it to deny that He is divine.
options.note: Jesus uses the psalm to defend, not deny, His divine sonship.

options.text: He uses it to argue that all people are gods in the same sense He is.
options.note: Jesus' argument distinguishes His unique sonship from the psalm's human judges, not equates them.

options.text: He quotes it to predict the fall of the Roman Empire.
options.note: Jesus applies the verse to His own identity, not to Roman political history.

questions: Every human authority in this psalm holds power only by God's delegation, verse 6-7. Where do you exercise authority, whether at home, work, or elsewhere, and how does knowing it is borrowed change how you use it?

God's charge against the judges is specific: defend the weak, fatherless, afflicted, and oppressed, verses 3-4. Who in your own circle of influence is weak or overlooked, and what would it look like to actively defend their cause?
The psalm says corrupted leadership makes 'the foundations of the earth' shake, verse 5. Where do you see the effects of injustice destabilizing trust and stability around you today?
Even those called 'gods' will 'die like mortals,' verse 7. How does remembering your own mortality shape the way you use whatever authority or influence you have been given?
The psalm ends with a prayer, 'arise, O God, judge the earth,' verse 8. What situation of injustice are you tempted to fix yourself rather than bringing honestly to God in prayer?

translationNotes.note: 'Gods' (elohim) here refers to human judges or rulers exercising delegated authority, as in Exodus 22:28, not to rival deities; the same Hebrew word is simply God's ordinary name elsewhere in the Psalter.
'How long will you judge unjustly' can also be rendered 'how long will you defend the unjust,' with the same basic charge of corrupted judgment either way.
'Sons of the Most High' identifies the judges' derived, God-given office and authority, the very phrase Jesus draws on in John 10:34-36 to defend His own unique divine sonship.

Footnote reference markers in verses 2 and 6 removed; no em/en dashes were present in the verse text. Verse count (8) and first/last verse wording checked against the section map. Sermon link verified this session: John Piper, 'Putting the Gods in Their Place' (desiringgod.org), an article by Piper directly expounding Psalm 82; no audio sermon on this exact psalm was found from the trusted cluster.
